items.mistake: Tuning λ by minimizing training loss
items.fix: Select λ via cross-validation or a held-out validation set on a proper scoring metric.
items.why: Training loss always favors smaller λ (less penalty); only out-of-sample error reveals the bias–variance sweet spot.
items.example: Training MSE is lowest at λ=0 but validation MSE is lowest at λ=1.0—picking 0 keeps overfitting.

items.mistake: Applying L1/L2 on unscaled linear features
items.fix: Standardize continuous predictors (fit scaler on train only) before penalized linear models.
items.why: Penalties sum over weight indices; features with larger numeric range escape effective regularization.
items.example: Income in dollars vs age in years: without scaling, age's coefficient gets penalized much more per unit of effect.

items.mistake: Cranking up λ when validation error is high but training error is also high
items.fix: Check the train–val pattern first; if both are high, reduce λ or increase model capacity instead of adding more regularization.
items.why: High train + high val indicates underfitting or excessive regularization—not overfitting.
items.example: At λ=100 both train and val accuracy sit at 55%; lowering λ to 0.1 raises both to 78%.

items.mistake: Expecting L1 to keep all correlated features
items.fix: Use L2 when correlated groups should be retained with shared shrinkage; use L1 when explicit sparsity is the goal.
items.why: L1's geometry tends to pick one member of a correlated set and zero the rest.
items.example: height_cm and height_in: L1 may keep one and zero the other despite both measuring height.
